([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Partito della Rifondazione Comunista*, abbr. **PRC**) is a [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism")[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) [political party in Italy](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Italy "List of political parties in Italy") that emerged from a split of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (PCI) in 1991. The party's secretary is [Maurizio Acerbo](/wiki/Maurizio_Acerbo "Maurizio Acerbo"), who replaced [Paolo Ferrero](/wiki/Paolo_Ferrero "Paolo Ferrero") in 2017. [Armando Cossutta](/wiki/Armando_Cossutta "Armando Cossutta") was the party's founder, while [Fausto Bertinotti](/wiki/Fausto_Bertinotti "Fausto Bertinotti") its longest-serving leader (1994–2008). The latter transformed the PRC from a traditional [communist party](/wiki/Communist_party "Communist party") into a collection of radical [social movements](/wiki/Social_movement "Social movement").

The **Communist Refoundation Party** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Partito della Rifondazione Comunista*, abbr. **PRC**) is a [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism")[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) [political party in Italy](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Italy "List of political parties in Italy") that emerged from a split of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (PCI) in 1991. The party's secretary is [Maurizio Acerbo](/wiki/Maurizio_Acerbo "Maurizio Acerbo"), who replaced [Paolo Ferrero](/wiki/Paolo_Ferrero "Paolo Ferrero") in 2017. [Armando Cossutta](/wiki/Armando_Cossutta "Armando Cossutta") was the party's founder, while [Fausto Bertinotti](/wiki/Fausto_Bertinotti "Fausto Bertinotti") its longest-serving leader (1994–2008). The latter transformed the PRC from a traditional [communist party](/wiki/Communist_party "Communist party") into a collection of radical [social movements](/wiki/Social_movement "Social movement").

The PRC is a member of the [Party of the European Left](/wiki/Party_of_the_European_Left "Party of the European Left") (PEL), of which Bertinotti was the inaugural president in 2004. The PRC has not been represented in the [Italian Parliament](/wiki/Italian_Parliament "Italian Parliament") since 2008, but had a [member](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") of the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"), [Eleonora Forenza](/wiki/Eleonora_Forenza "Eleonora Forenza"), who sat with the [European United Left–Nordic Green Left](/wiki/European_United_Left%E2%80%93Nordic_Green_Left "European United Left–Nordic Green Left") (GUE/NGL) group in 2014–2019.

In February 1991, when the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (PCI) was transformed into the [Democratic Party of the Left](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_the_Left "Democratic Party of the Left") (PDS) under the leadership of [Achille Occhetto](/wiki/Achille_Occhetto "Achille Occhetto"), left-wing dissidents led by [Armando Cossutta](/wiki/Armando_Cossutta "Armando Cossutta") launched the Movement for Communist Refoundation. Hardliners in PCI were not happy about the changes made inside the party after the fall of the Iron Curtain. Later that year, [Proletarian Democracy](/wiki/Proletarian_Democracy "Proletarian Democracy") (DP), a far-left outfit, dissolved itself so that its members could join the PCI dissidents and form a united front composed of all Italian communists. In December, the PRC was officially founded and [Sergio Garavini](/wiki/Sergio_Garavini "Sergio Garavini") was elected secretary. In the [1992 general election](/wiki/1992_Italian_general_election "1992 Italian general election"), the party obtained 5.6% of the vote.

Garavini resigned from his role as secretary in June 1993 and was replaced by [Fausto Bertinotti](/wiki/Fausto_Bertinotti "Fausto Bertinotti"), a trade unionist of the [Italian General Confederation of Labour](/wiki/Italian_General_Confederation_of_Labour "Italian General Confederation of Labour") (CGIL) who had left the PDS only a few months before. In the [1994 general election](/wiki/1994_Italian_general_election "1994 Italian general election"), the PRC was part of the PDS-led [Alliance of Progressives](/wiki/Alliance_of_Progressives "Alliance of Progressives") and obtained 6.1% of the vote. In June 1995, a splinter group led by [Lucio Magri](/wiki/Lucio_Magri "Lucio Magri") and [Famiano Crucianelli](/wiki/Famiano_Crucianelli "Famiano Crucianelli") formed the [Movement of Unitarian Communists](/wiki/Movement_of_Unitarian_Communists "Movement of Unitarian Communists") (MCU), which would eventually merge with the PDS, being one of the founding members of the [Democrats of the Left](/wiki/Democrats_of_the_Left "Democrats of the Left") (DS) in February 1998.

### Bertinotti vs. Cossutta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communist_Refoundation_Party&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Bertinotti vs. Cossutta")]

The leadership of Bertinotti was a turning point for the party, which jumped to 8.6% of vote in the [1996 general election](/wiki/1996_Italian_general_election "1996 Italian general election"), fought by the party in a loose alliance with [The Olive Tree](/wiki/The_Olive_Tree_(Italy) "The Olive Tree (Italy)"), the major [centre-left coalition](/wiki/Centre-left_coalition_(Italy) "Centre-left coalition (Italy)") whose dominant partner was the PDS. After the election, the PRC decided to externally support the [first cabinet](/wiki/Prodi_I_Cabinet "Prodi I Cabinet") led by [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi").

Tensions soon arose within the coalition and the party. In October 1998 the PRC was divided between those who wanted to stop supporting Prodi's government, led by Bertinotti; and those who wanted to continue the alliance, led by Cossutta, the party's president. The central committee endorsed Bertinotti's line, but Cossutta and his followers decided to ignore this line and to support Prodi. The votes of the *cossuttiani* were not enough and the government lost a confidence vote in Parliament.

The dissidents, who controlled the majority of deputies and senators, split and formed a rival communist party, the [Party of Italian Communists](/wiki/Party_of_Italian_Communists "Party of Italian Communists") (PdCI), which would soon join the [first cabinet](/wiki/D%27Alema_I_Cabinet "D'Alema I Cabinet") led by [Massimo D'Alema](/wiki/Massimo_D%27Alema "Massimo D'Alema"), the leader of the DS, who replaced Prodi and became the first post-communist to hold the job of [Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy").

Deprived of most of its parliamentary representation, the PRC fought for its existence and voters supported it rather than the PdCI, both in the [1999 European Parliament election](/wiki/1999_European_Parliament_election_in_Italy "1999 European Parliament election in Italy") (4.3% to 2.0%) and the [2001 general election](/wiki/2001_Italian_general_election "2001 Italian general election") (5.0% to 1.7%).

### Renewal and heyday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communist_Refoundation_Party&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Renewal and heyday")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Bertinotti_ivcon.jpg/250px-Bertinotti_ivcon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bertinotti_ivcon.jpg)

PRC's long-time leader [Fausto Bertinotti](/wiki/Fausto_Bertinotti "Fausto Bertinotti") at a party congress in 1999

Despite competition from the PdCI, the PRC confirmed its status as Italy's largest communist party. Having been left by most traditional communists, it also started to enlarge its scope aiming at becoming a collector of radical [social movements](/wiki/Social_movement "Social movement") and, foremost, the main representative of the [anti-globalization movement](/wiki/Anti-globalization_movement "Anti-globalization movement") in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). The PRC also forged new alliances at the European level and was instrumental in the foundation of the [Party of the European Left](/wiki/Party_of_the_European_Left "Party of the European Left") in May 2004.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In October 2004, the PRC re-joined the centre-left coalition, once again led by Prodi. In April 2005, [Nichi Vendola](/wiki/Nichi_Vendola "Nichi Vendola"), an openly [gay](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") politician and one of the emerging leaders of the party, won a primary election and was elected president of traditionally conservative [southern region](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy") of [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia"), becoming the only regional president ever belonging to the PRC.

In the [2006 general election](/wiki/2006_Italian_general_election "2006 Italian general election"), the PRC was part of [The Union](/wiki/The_Union_(Italy) "The Union (Italy)"), which won narrowly over the [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right "Centre-right") [House of Freedoms](/wiki/House_of_Freedoms "House of Freedoms") coalition and the party obtained 5.8%. After the election, Bertinotti was elected [President of the Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_the_Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "List of Presidents of the Chamber of Deputies (Italy)") and replaced by [Franco Giordano](/wiki/Franco_Giordano "Franco Giordano") as secretary. Additionally, for the first time it entered a government by joining the [Prodi II Cabinet](/wiki/Prodi_II_Cabinet "Prodi II Cabinet"), with [Paolo Ferrero](/wiki/Paolo_Ferrero "Paolo Ferrero") Minister of Social Solidarity and seven undersecretaries. The decision to participate in the coalition government and vote to refinance the Italian military presence in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") and send troops to [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") attracted criticism from sectors of the European far-left[[12]](#cite_note-12) and provoked the splits of several groups from the ranks of his own party, notably including the [Workers' Communist Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Communist_Party_(Italy) "Workers' Communist Party (Italy)"), the [Communist Alternative Party](/wiki/Communist_Alternative_Party "Communist Alternative Party") and [Critical Left](/wiki/Critical_Left "Critical Left"). Prodi, whose majority was weak and fragmented, resigned in January 2008.

### Crisis, splits and decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communist_Refoundation_Party&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Crisis, splits and decline")]

For the [2008 general election](/wiki/2008_Italian_general_election "2008 Italian general election"), the PRC formed a joint list named [Rainbow Left](/wiki/The_Left_%E2%80%93_The_Rainbow "The Left – The Rainbow") (SA) with the PdCI, the [Federation of the Greens](/wiki/Federation_of_the_Greens "Federation of the Greens") and the [Democratic Left](/wiki/Democratic_Left_(Italy) "Democratic Left (Italy)") under Bertinotti's leadership. SA obtained a mere 3.1% (compared to 10.2% won by the constituent parties individually two years before) and no seats. Consequently, Bertinotti quit politics and Giordano resigned and after that some *[bertinottiani](/wiki/Bertinottiani "Bertinottiani")*, led by Ferrero and [Giovanni Russo Spena](/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_Russo_Spena&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giovanni Russo Spena (page does not exist)") (both former [Proletarian Democracy](/wiki/Proletarian_Democracy "Proletarian Democracy") members), had forged an alliance with former *cossuttiani*.

At the July 2008 congress, the PRC was highly divided around ideological and regional lines with Vendola, the *bertinottiani'*s standard-bearer, accusing northern delegates of having absorbed *[leghismo](/wiki/Lega_Nord "Lega Nord")* and stating that it was the end of the party as he knew it. The internal left-wing (which wanted to return to PRC's original communist project) finally prevailed over the bulk of *bertinottiani* (who insisted on the creation of a broader left-wing party) and Ferrero was elected secretary by the central committee with 50.5%.[[13]](#cite_note-corriere.it-13)

In January 2009, the faction around Vendola and Giordano, silently supported by Bertinotti, left the PRC and launched the [Movement for the Left](/wiki/Movement_for_the_Left "Movement for the Left") (MpS), aimed at forming a broader left-wing party,[[14]](#cite_note-14) which would eventually be [Left Ecology Freedom](/wiki/Left_Ecology_Freedom "Left Ecology Freedom") (SEL).

### Left-wing alliances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communist_Refoundation_Party&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Left-wing alliances")]

In the [2009 European Parliament election](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_Italy "2009 European Parliament election in Italy") the PRC ran with the PdCI and minor groups within the [Anticapitalist and Communist List](/wiki/Anticapitalist_and_Communist_List "Anticapitalist and Communist List"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) obtaining 3.4% of the vote and no [MEPs](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament"). In April 2009 the list was transformed into the [Federation of the Left](/wiki/Federation_of_the_Left "Federation of the Left"),[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) which would be disbanded by the end of 2012[[18]](#cite_note-18) and officially dissolved in 2015.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In the [2013 general election](/wiki/2013_Italian_general_election "2013 Italian general election") the PRC ran within [Civil Revolution](/wiki/Civil_Revolution "Civil Revolution") along with the PdCI, the Greens, [Italy of Values](/wiki/Italy_of_Values "Italy of Values") and minor groups, obtaining 2.2% and no seats.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In the [2014 European Parliament election](/wiki/2014_European_Parliament_election_in_Italy "2014 European Parliament election in Italy") the PRC was part of [The Other Europe](/wiki/The_Other_Europe "The Other Europe"), which obtained 4.0% of the vote and three MEPs, including PRC's [Eleonora Forenza](/wiki/Eleonora_Forenza "Eleonora Forenza").

In April 2017 Ferrero was replaced as secretary by [Maurizio Acerbo](/wiki/Maurizio_Acerbo "Maurizio Acerbo"), a former member of the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "Chamber of Deputies (Italy)").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In the [2018 general election](/wiki/2018_Italian_general_election "2018 Italian general election") the PRC was part of the [Power to the People](/wiki/Power_to_the_People_(Italy) "Power to the People (Italy)") (PaP) electoral list,[[24]](#cite_note-Today-24)[[25]](#cite_note-ilmanifesto-25) which obtained 1.1% of the vote and no seats.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In 2020–2021 the party was briefly represented in the Senate by [Paola Nugnes](/w/index.php?title=Paola_Nugnes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Paola Nugnes (page does not exist)"), a splinter from the [Five Star Movement](/wiki/Five_Star_Movement "Five Star Movement") who later joined [Italian Left](/wiki/Italian_Left "Italian Left") (SI).[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In the [2019 European Parliament election](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election_in_Italy "2019 European Parliament election in Italy") the PRC was part of [The Left](/wiki/The_Left_(Italy) "The Left (Italy)") electoral list, which obtained 1.8% and no seats.

In February 2022 the party formed a joint sub-group with PaP in the Chamber of Deputies' Mixed Group.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In June 2022 the same happened in the Senate,[[31]](#cite_note-31) and senator Nugnes returned to the party. In the run-up of the [2022 general election](/wiki/2022_Italian_general_election "2022 Italian general election") the PRC was a founding member of the [People's Union](/wiki/People%27s_Union_(Italy) "People's Union (Italy)") (UP), a left-wing electoral list led by [Luigi de Magistris](/wiki/Luigi_de_Magistris_(politician) "Luigi de Magistris (politician)").

In the run-up of the February 2025 congress the proposition put forward by incumbent secretary Acerbo obtained 50.7% of the vote of party members in local congresses, while a large majority led by Ferrero won 49.3%.[[32]](#cite_note-32) As a result, Acerbo was narrowly re-elected secretary.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Factions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Communist_Refoundation_Party&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Factions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/PRC_flags_in_Rome.JPG/250px-PRC_flags_in_Rome.JPG)](/wiki/File:PRC_flags_in_Rome.JPG)

A PRC rally in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), 2007

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/PRC_office_Venice.jpg/250px-PRC_office_Venice.jpg)](/wiki/File:PRC_office_Venice.jpg)

PRC headquarters in [Castello](/wiki/Castello,_Venice "Castello, Venice"), [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice")

The majority of the party following the October 2004 congress was led by [Fausto Bertinotti](/wiki/Fausto_Bertinotti "Fausto Bertinotti") (59.2%) and viewed the PRC as the representative of the [anti-globalization movement](/wiki/Anti-globalization_movement "Anti-globalization movement") in Italy. Other factions strongly opposed Bertinotti's innovations. These included the hard-line traditionalist [Being Communists](/wiki/Being_Communists "Being Communists") (26.2%) which was composed of former followers of [Armando Cossutta](/wiki/Armando_Cossutta "Armando Cossutta") as well as the [Trotskyists](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism") of [Critical Left](/wiki/Critical_Left "Critical Left"), [Communist Project](/wiki/Communist_Project "Communist Project") and [HammerSickle](/wiki/HammerSickle "HammerSickle") (14.6% together). Communist Project, which opposed the party's participation in the [Prodi II Cabinet](/wiki/Prodi_II_Cabinet "Prodi II Cabinet"), unfolded shortly after the [2006 general election](/wiki/2006_Italian_general_election "2006 Italian general election"). A group led by [Francesco Ricci](/w/index.php?title=Francesco_Ricci_(politician)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Francesco Ricci (politician) (page does not exist)") established the [Communist Alternative Party](/wiki/Communist_Alternative_Party "Communist Alternative Party"), others, led by the [Trotskyist](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism") [Marco Ferrando](/wiki/Marco_Ferrando "Marco Ferrando"), formed the [Workers' Communist Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Communist_Party_(Italy) "Workers' Communist Party (Italy)"), while a tiny minority chose to stay in the party and launched [Countercurrent](/wiki/Countercurrent_(Italy,_2006) "Countercurrent (Italy, 2006)").

In February 2007, senator [Franco Turigliatto](/wiki/Franco_Turigliatto "Franco Turigliatto") of Critical Left, led by [Salvatore Cannavò](/w/index.php?title=Salvatore_Cannav%C3%B2&action=edit&redlink=1 "Salvatore Cannavò (page does not exist)"), voted twice against the government's foreign policy, leading [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi") to temporarily resign from [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy"). In April, Turigliatto was expelled from the party and Critical Left was suspended from it, leading to its final split and establishment as a party in December. Turigliatto's ejection was supported also by [Claudio Grassi](/wiki/Claudio_Grassi_(politician) "Claudio Grassi (politician)") (leader of Being Communists) and this caused a break-up of the faction. A group led by [Fosco Giannini](/w/index.php?title=Fosco_Giannini&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fosco Giannini (page does not exist)") launched an alternative faction named [The Ernesto](/wiki/The_Ernesto "The Ernesto") (from the eponymous communist publication), but it would suffer the 2008 split of [Communist Left](/w/index.php?title=Communist_Left_(Italy)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Communist Left (Italy) (page does not exist)"), which would splinter in 2011 into Communist Left and Communists Together/[The Future City](/w/index.php?title=The_Future_City&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Future City (page does not exist)").

Following the severe defeat of the party in the [2008 general election](/wiki/2008_Italian_general_election "2008 Italian general election"), a group of *[bertinottiani](/wiki/Bertinottiani "Bertinottiani")* composed mainly of former members of [Proletarian Democracy](/wiki/Proletarian_Democracy "Proletarian Democracy") and led by [Paolo Ferrero](/wiki/Paolo_Ferrero "Paolo Ferrero") and [Giovanni Russo Spena](/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_Russo_Spena&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giovanni Russo Spena (page does not exist)") allied with the other minority factions, notably including Being Communists, to force [Franco Giordano](/wiki/Franco_Giordano "Franco Giordano")'s resignation from secretary. Subsequently, in the July congress Ferrero's and Grassi's [Refoundation in Movement](/wiki/Refoundation_in_Movement "Refoundation in Movement") motion (40.1%) faced the bulk of *bertinottiani*, who organized themselves around a motion named "Manifesto for the Refoundation" (47.6%) with [Nichi Vendola](/wiki/Nichi_Vendola "Nichi Vendola") as standard-bearer. Giannini's The Ernesto and Countercurrent (7.7%), [Claudio Bellotti](/w/index.php?title=Claudio_Bellotti&action=edit&redlink=1 "Claudio Bellotti (page does not exist)")'s HammerSickle (3.2%) and a minor group of former *bertinottiani* called "Disarm, Renew, Refound" (1.5%) joined forces with the Ferrero-Grassi group. Vendola, defeated by Ferrero, announced the creation of a new minority faction, [Refoundation for the Left](/wiki/Refoundation_for_the_Left "Refoundation for the Left") (RpS).[[13]](#cite_note-corriere.it-13)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

RpS finally left the party in 2009 to form the [Movement for the Left](/wiki/Movement_for_the_Left "Movement for the Left") (MpS), but some of its members, led by [Augusto Rocchi](/w/index.php?title=Augusto_Rocchi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Augusto Rocchi (page does not exist)"), decided to stay in the PRC[[35]](#cite_note-35) and launched [To the Left with Refoundation](/wiki/To_the_Left_with_Refoundation "To the Left with Refoundation").[[36]](#cite_note-36) However, the alliance between Ferrero and the traditionalists did not last. The Ernesto joined the PdCI in 2011 while Being Communists divided in two groups, both eventually quitting the party. One group joined SEL in 2014[[37]](#cite_note-37) and was later merged into the [Democratic and Progressive Movement](/wiki/Article_One_(political_party) "Article One (political party)") (MDP) in 2016;[[38]](#cite_note-38) a second, larger group (including Grassi) participated in the foundation of SEL's successor, [Italian Left](/wiki/Italian_Left "Italian Left") (SI).[[39]](#cite_note-39) Indeed, between 2014 and 2016 all of Being Communists quit the PRC and finally ended in SI.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) In early 2016 the Trotskyist [Left Class Revolution](/wiki/Left_Class_Revolution "Left Class Revolution") left the party too,[[42]](#cite_note-42) while Countercurrent seemed no longer active.

In the 2017 congress, two motions were presented by Ferrero and [Eleonora Forenza](/wiki/Eleonora_Forenza "Eleonora Forenza"), respectively.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The coalition of factions led by Ferrero prevailed with the vote of 71.5% of party members.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Consequently, [Maurizio Acerbo](/wiki/Maurizio_Acerbo "Maurizio Acerbo"), supported by Ferrero,[[45]](#cite_note-45) was elected secretary by the central committee.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

* [Secretary](/wiki/Secretary_of_the_Communist_Refoundation_Party "Secretary of the Communist Refoundation Party"): [Sergio Garavini](/wiki/Sergio_Garavini "Sergio Garavini") (1991–1993), [Fausto Bertinotti](/wiki/Fausto_Bertinotti "Fausto Bertinotti") (1994–2006), [Franco Giordano](/wiki/Franco_Giordano "Franco Giordano") (2006–2008), [Paolo Ferrero](/wiki/Paolo_Ferrero "Paolo Ferrero") (2008–2017), [Maurizio Acerbo](/wiki/Maurizio_Acerbo "Maurizio Acerbo") (2017–present)
  + Coordinator: [Walter De Cesaris](/w/index.php?title=Walter_De_Cesaris&action=edit&redlink=1 "Walter De Cesaris (page does not exist)") (2004–2008), [Nando Mainardi](/w/index.php?title=Nando_Mainardi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nando Mainardi (page does not exist)") (2014–2017), [Stefano Galieni](/w/index.php?title=Stefano_Galieni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stefano Galieni (page does not exist)") (2017–present)
* President: [Armando Cossutta](/wiki/Armando_Cossutta "Armando Cossutta") (1991–1998)[[a]](#cite_note-51)
* Leader in the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "Chamber of Deputies (Italy)"): [Lucio Magri](/wiki/Lucio_Magri "Lucio Magri") (1992–1994), [Famiano Crucianelli](/wiki/Famiano_Crucianelli "Famiano Crucianelli") (1994–1995), [Oliviero Diliberto](/wiki/Oliviero_Diliberto "Oliviero Diliberto") (1995–1998), [Franco Giordano](/wiki/Franco_Giordano "Franco Giordano") (1998–2006), [Gennaro Migliore](/wiki/Gennaro_Migliore "Gennaro Migliore") (2006–2008)
* Leader in the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Republic_(Italy) "Senate of the Republic (Italy)"): [Lucio Libertini](/w/index.php?title=Lucio_Libertini&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lucio Libertini (page does not exist)") (1992–1993), [Ersilia Salvato](/w/index.php?title=Ersilia_Salvato&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ersilia Salvato (page does not exist)") (1993–1995), [Fausto Marchetti](/wiki/Fausto_Marchetti "Fausto Marchetti") (1995–1996), [Luigi Marino](/w/index.php?title=Luigi_Marino&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luigi Marino (page does not exist)") (1996–1998), [Giovanni Russo Spena](/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_Russo_Spena&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giovanni Russo Spena (page does not exist)") (1998–2001), [Giorgio Malentacchi](/wiki/Giorgio_Malentacchi "Giorgio Malentacchi") (2001–2002), [Luigi Malabarba](/w/index.php?title=Luigi_Malabarba&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luigi Malabarba (page does not exist)") (2002–2006), [Giovanni Russo Spena](/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_Russo_Spena&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giovanni Russo Spena (page does not exist)") (2006–2008)
* Leader in the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"): [Luigi Vinci](/w/index.php?title=Luigi_Vinci&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luigi Vinci (page does not exist)") (1994–2004), [Roberto Musacchio](/wiki/Roberto_Musacchio "Roberto Musacchio") (2004–2009), [Eleonora Forenza](/wiki/Eleonora_Forenza "Eleonora Forenza") (2014–2019)
